title: AI Delivery Team — Project Intake
description: Fill this file before running /start-project. The orchestrator reads it to assign agents, set the output directory, and produce a delivery plan.

$schema.description: Points to the JSON schema for VS Code autocomplete. Do not edit.

name.description: Kebab-case project identifier. Becomes the output directory name: output/{name}/. Example: customer-portal, analytics-dashboard, auth-service.

display_name.description: Human-readable title shown in status reports and delivery summaries. Example: Customer Portal.

description.description: 2-3 sentences describing what this project is, who it is for, and the core problem it solves. Used by the business-analyst-agent as the starting context for requirements.
examples: A self-service portal for enterprise customers to manage their subscriptions, view invoices, and contact support. Replaces the current manual email-based process.
An internal dashboard for the ops team to monitor real-time order processing metrics and trigger manual interventions when automation fails.

type.description: Project category. Used by the orchestrator to determine the default agent set. web-app: full frontend + backend + DB. api: backend only. data-pipeline: ETL/ML focus. mobile: React Native. ai-feature: LLM/RAG feature added to an existing product. internal-tool: lightweight ops/admin tool.

stack.description: Technology choices. Each value maps directly to which agents are assigned and which path-scoped rules activate.

frontend.description: Frontend framework. Activates frontend-engineer agent and loads frontend-patterns.md rules. Use 'none' for API-only projects.

backend.description: Backend framework. Activates backend-engineer or full-stack-engineer. Loads api-patterns.md rules.

database.description: Primary database. Activates database.md path-scoped rules. If set, backend-engineer handles schema design and migrations.

auth.description: Authentication strategy. Informs backend-engineer on which patterns to use from security.md.

hosting.description: Target cloud or hosting platform. Triggers devops-cloud-engineer assignment for infra setup and CI/CD.

ai_ml.description: Set to true if the project requires LLM, RAG, embeddings, chatbot, ML models, or data pipelines. Triggers genai-llm-engineer or data-ml-engineer assignment.

ai_ml_type.description: Required when ai_ml is true. 'llm-rag' triggers genai-llm-engineer (chatbot, RAG, prompt engineering, embeddings). 'ml-model' triggers data-ml-engineer (predictions, classification, data pipelines).

requirements.description: What to build, what not to build, and performance/security expectations.

features.description: List of core features to build. The business-analyst-agent converts each item into user stories with acceptance criteria. Be specific — vague features become vague stories.
examples: User registration and login with email/password
Dashboard showing order history with filtering by date and status
Role-based access: admin can view all users, regular user sees own data only

out_of_scope.description: Explicit exclusions. Anything listed here will not be built in this project. Prevents scope creep and stops agents from building things not asked for.
examples: Payment processing (handled by existing Stripe integration)

non_functional.description: Performance, scale, and security requirements. Directly influences agent decisions on architecture, indexing, and security review depth.

expected_users.description: Rough scale estimate. Affects infra sizing (devops-cloud-engineer) and caching/pagination decisions (backend-engineer).

performance.description: Specific performance targets. Example: 'API p95 < 200ms, dashboard load < 2s'. Used as acceptance criteria for backend-engineer and devops-cloud-engineer.
examples: Batch job processes 1M rows in < 10 minutes

security_level.description: standard: normal security.md rules apply. high: mandatory security review on every PR, penetration test before release, stricter auth requirements. Use 'high' for projects handling financial data, PII, healthcare, or regulated industries.

timeline.description: Deadline and milestones. The orchestrator validates feasibility against the feature list and flags risk if scope is unrealistic.

deadline.description: Target completion date in ISO format (YYYY-MM-DD) or a relative estimate like '6 weeks'. The orchestrator will flag if the feature list is unrealistic for this timeline.

milestones.description: Optional intermediate milestones. Useful for phased delivery or stakeholder checkpoints.

team.description: Optional overrides for agent assignment. Leave both arrays empty to let the orchestrator decide automatically based on stack and project type.

agents_needed.description: Force-include specific agents. Leave empty for auto-assignment. Only use this if you know you need something the orchestrator wouldn't pick automatically.

skip_agents.description: Force-exclude specific agents even if the orchestrator would normally assign them. Example: skip devops-cloud-engineer if infra already exists.

context.description: Additional context agents need before starting work.

existing_codebase.description: Set to true if agents will be working inside or alongside an existing codebase. When true, all agents will read the existing code before writing anything new.

codebase_notes.description: Required when existing_codebase is true. Brief description of the existing codebase: what it does, its main tech stack, known quirks, and where new work fits in.
examples: Existing Node.js Express API with PostgreSQL. Auth is handled by a separate internal service at /api/auth. New features must use the existing User model in src/models/user.ts.

reference_docs.description: Paths to docs, specs, or files agents should read before starting. Relative to repo root. Example: PRDs, API specs, architecture diagrams.

constraints.description: Hard rules every agent must follow throughout this project. These are enforced constraints, not suggestions. Example: must use internal auth service, cannot use third-party analytics, must support IE11.
examples: Must use the internal auth service at auth.internal — no custom JWT implementation
No external analytics or tracking SDKs
All API responses must be logged to the company audit log service

output.description: Controls where agents write generated project files. Normally auto-set — only override if you need a custom path.

dir.description: Output directory for all generated project files. Auto-set to output/{project.name}/ if left empty. All agents write here per the coding-standards.md rule. Override only if needed.
